title: Check Washing Scam: How to Protect Your Checks and Bank Account
excerpt: Learn how check washing scams work, the biggest warning signs, and the best ways to protect checks, mail, and your bank account.
content:
Check washing is an old form of fraud that still causes real damage. Even though many people pay bills online now, paper checks remain a target because scammers can steal them, alter them, and drain money from your account before you realize what happened.

If you still mail checks for rent, utilities, donations, taxes, or small business payments, this is a security risk worth understanding. The good news is that a few practical habits can make you a much harder target.

In this guide, you will learn what a check washing scam is, how criminals pull it off, the warning signs to watch for, and the best ways to protect your checks, your mail, and your bank account.

## What is a check washing scam?

A check washing scam happens when a thief steals a legitimate paper check and removes or changes important details, usually the payee name and sometimes the amount. The scammer then deposits or cashes the altered check for their own benefit.

According to the U.S. Postal Inspection Service and the OCC, criminals often steal checks from personal mailboxes, blue collection boxes, apartment mailrooms, or outgoing office mail. They may use chemicals to remove ink, or they may use digital editing and counterfeit techniques after capturing the check details.

The result is the same: money leaves your account based on a check you really wrote, but not in the way you intended.

## Why check washing is still a problem

People sometimes assume paper check fraud disappeared years ago. It did not. Check fraud remains attractive to criminals for a few reasons:

- Many people still send checks by mail
- Stolen checks can reveal your name, address, bank routing number, and account number
- Some altered checks are not noticed until days later
- Mail theft can happen in batches, giving fraudsters many targets at once
- Victims often do not review cleared check images right away

If you only look at your account balance and never inspect check details, a scammer may have more time than you think.

## How check washing scams usually work

Most check washing schemes follow a simple pattern.

### 1. The thief steals a check

This often happens after someone drops a check in an unsecured mailbox or puts outgoing mail up for pickup overnight. Some criminals target postal collection boxes or building mailrooms.

### 2. The check is altered

The scammer may remove the original payee name, change the amount, or use the account information to create a counterfeit check. In some cases, the original signature remains while key payment details are changed.

### 3. The altered check is deposited or cashed

The fraudster tries to move the money quickly, sometimes through another account or a fake identity, before the victim notices.

### 4. The victim discovers the loss later

You may notice a higher amount than expected, a different payee, or a check number that cleared in a suspicious way. By then, the cleanup process has already become harder.

## Warning signs of check washing or check fraud

Watch for these red flags in your bank account and mail activity:

- A check you mailed never reaches the intended recipient
- A check clears for more money than you wrote
- The payee name on the cleared image looks wrong
- Check numbers appear out of order or missing
- You see checks you do not recognize at all
- Your mailbox shows signs of tampering or theft
- Bills or financial mail suddenly stop arriving

If anything looks off, contact your bank quickly. Speed matters with check fraud.

## How to prevent check washing scams

You cannot control every risk, but you can reduce the odds significantly.

### Use online bill pay when possible

If a secure electronic payment option is available, it usually lowers your exposure to mail theft and altered paper checks. This is one of the easiest ways to cut risk.

### Avoid leaving outgoing checks in your home mailbox

If you must mail a check, do not leave it sitting in an unlocked residential mailbox for hours. Bring it inside the post office when possible, or use a secure drop location during the day rather than overnight.

### Use a gel pen with dark permanent ink

Banks and fraud experts often recommend gel pens because the ink is harder to wash away than standard ballpoint ink. It is not a perfect defense, but it can make alteration more difficult.

### Fill in every line carefully

Write clearly and avoid blank spaces that make it easier for someone to add words or numbers. For example, complete the amount line fully and draw a line after the written amount if you normally do that.

### Track every check you send

Keep a record of the date, check number, payee, and exact amount. If something goes wrong, having a clean record helps you spot the fraud faster and explain it to your bank.

### Review cleared check images

Do not rely only on your account balance. Look at the actual image of each cleared check in your online banking portal. This is one of the fastest ways to catch a changed payee or amount.

### Secure your mailbox

A locking mailbox, secure building mail area, or prompt mail collection can reduce your exposure. If you travel often, avoid letting sensitive mail pile up.

### Order checks carefully

If you still use paper checks regularly, keep them in a safe place at home. Missing blank checks are a major problem because scammers can use them for counterfeiting or forgery.

## What to do if you think a check was washed

If you suspect check fraud, do not wait and hope it sorts itself out.

### Contact your bank immediately

Report the suspicious transaction and ask what fraud steps they want you to follow. The bank may place a stop payment, open an investigation, close the account, or move you to a new account number depending on what happened.

### Save records and screenshots

Download the check image, transaction details, and any messages connected to the payment. If the intended recipient says they never got the check, save that information too.

### Report mail theft if it may be involved

If you think the check was stolen from the mail, report it to the U.S. Postal Inspection Service. That can help document the incident and support a broader fraud investigation.

### Review the rest of your account activity

One stolen check can expose enough information for more fraud. Review recent transactions, watch for unauthorized ACH activity, and consider whether you need a new checking account number.

### Update any automatic payments if needed

If your bank closes the compromised account, make a list of direct deposits and recurring bills so you can switch them over quickly.

## Check washing vs other types of check fraud

Check washing is only one version of check fraud. Others include:

- Forged signatures
- Counterfeit checks created from stolen account details
- Stolen blank checks
- Fake overpayment or fake cashier's check scams

The key difference with check washing is that the scam often starts with a real check you actually wrote. That can make it more surprising and harder to catch if you are not reviewing images closely.

## Who is most at risk?

Anyone who mails paper checks can be targeted, but the risk can be higher for:

- Older adults who pay many bills by mail
- Small business owners who send vendor checks
- Landlords or property managers handling paper rent payments
- People using unsecured apartment or curbside mailboxes
- Anyone who sends tax payments or large checks through the mail

If paper checks are part of your routine, building safer habits matters.

## Frequently asked questions

### What is check washing?

Check washing is when a criminal steals a real check and alters the payee, amount, or other details to redirect money.

### Can a bank refund money from a washed check?

Sometimes, but policies and timelines vary. Report the fraud immediately and follow your bank's dispute process. The faster you act, the better.

### Is mailing checks still safe?

Mailing checks carries more risk than many electronic payment methods, especially if you use unsecured mail drop points. If you must mail a check, use the safest drop method available and monitor the payment closely.

### Does using a gel pen really help?

It can help because gel ink is generally harder to remove than some other inks, but it is not a guarantee. You still need safe mailing and account monitoring habits.

### What should I do if a check I mailed never arrives?

Contact the recipient, review your account, and talk to your bank about whether a stop payment makes sense. If theft is possible, report it quickly.

## Bottom line

Check washing scams work when thieves get access to your checks and you do not catch the fraud fast enough. The best defenses are simple: use electronic payments when you can, mail checks more securely, write them carefully, and review cleared check images instead of only watching your balance.

If you still use paper checks, a few extra minutes of caution can protect a lot of money and hassle.
